graderReview.content:
============================================================
COMP421 Midterm Exam - LLM Grading Results
============================================================

RESULTS SUMMARY
---------------
Passed: 6/6 problems (97%)
Points: 97/100

DETAILED RESULTS
----------------
[PASS] Problem 1: Short Definitions (10/10 pts)
       This is a strong definitions section. Each term is defined accurately and in the intended OS context, with no material misconceptions. Your semaphore definition captures counting behavior, race condition correctly emphasizes interleaving dependence, and supervisor mode correctly identifies privileged execution.

[PASS] Problem 2: Synchronization Using a Monitor (22/25 pts)
       Your monitor is largely correct and demonstrates good understanding of Mesa monitors and buffer invariants. The key issue is fairness robustness under Mesa wakeup behavior: ticket counters alone do not guarantee the intended FIFO service unless wakeups are coordinated so the correct waiter is actually resumed. With a single condition variable and plain `signal`, the wrong waiter can wake and go back to sleep repeatedly, creating potential starvation/liveness risk under adversarial scheduling. Also, head-of-line blocking from strict producer FIFO limits concurrency compared to policies that allow feasible requests when safe. Overall, strong work with a modest deduction for liveness/concurrency edge cases.
       Errors: Ticket order is not fully enforced by Mesa condition signaling because `signal()` may wake a non-front waiter; with only single condition variables and no directed wakeup/broadcast strategy, the front eligible waiter can be repeatedly skipped.; Strict producer ticket ordering can unnecessarily block feasible producers (e.g., head needs 2 while next needs 1 and one slot is free), reducing concurrency below the requested maximum.

[PASS] Problem 3: Synchronization Using Semaphores (25/25 pts)
       This is a correct semaphore-based design with clear invariants. `EnterRoom` enqueues requests and blocks until resources are reserved, and `LeaveRoom` replenishes and grants queued requests safely under mutual exclusion. The queue-based wakeup logic is coherent and addresses starvation concerns. This is a solid full-credit solution.

[PASS] Problem 4: The Critical Section Problem (15/15 pts)
       Your conclusions match the reference evaluation, and your reasoning is substantively correct. You correctly identify that bounded waiting fails via a plausible repeated scheduling pattern while preserving mutual exclusion and progress.

[PASS] Problem 5: Compaction (10/10 pts)
       Computation and final table are fully correct. You applied the compaction direction exactly as asked (coalesced free space at low addresses) and produced consistent BASE/LIMIT values.

[PASS] Problem 6: Hierarchical Page Tables (15/15 pts)
       This is a correct and detailed algorithmic answer. You show how to access L2 tables from L1 entries, compare all relevant PTE fields, and perform in-place pointer rewrites to share subtrees while preserving behavior. The correctness argument is sound and matches the required objective.

OVERALL COMMENTS
----------------
Excellent submission overall with consistently strong technical correctness across all six questions. Definitions, semaphore design, critical-section analysis, compaction arithmetic, and hierarchical page-table procedure were all high quality. The only notable weakness was in Question 2, where Mesa monitor wakeup behavior can undermine the intended ticket fairness/liveness guarantees and strict FIFO among producers can reduce achievable concurrency.

============================================================
